questions.q: A Compare-and-Swap (CAS) operation atomically compares a memory value with an expected value, and if they match, swaps in a new value. CAS on a shared counter ensures that only one thread's increment is visible. What happens if two threads call CAS simultaneously with the same expected value?
options: Both increments succeed and the counter is incremented by 2
Only one CAS succeeds and increments the counter by 1; the other sees a mismatch and must retry
The processor serializes the operations, ensuring exactly one increment and one retry in order
The counter is incremented by 2, and both threads are notified of success
questions.explanation: CAS is atomic: exactly one of the two operations succeeds. The first one to execute succeeds and updates the counter; the second sees that the current value no longer matches the expected value (because the first thread changed it) and must retry with the new value. This is the basis of lock-free algorithms—retries are required when contention occurs.

questions.q: A lock-free data structure guarantees that at least one thread always makes progress. Which statement correctly distinguishes lock-free from wait-free?
options: Lock-free and wait-free are synonymous terms
Lock-free allows indefinite blocking of individual threads, but wait-free guarantees that every thread makes progress within a bounded number of steps
Wait-free algorithms use locks optimistically, while lock-free use locks pessimistically
Lock-free uses atomic operations, while wait-free uses only mutexes
questions.explanation: Lock-free means the system as a whole always makes progress (no deadlock), but individual threads might starve (spin forever). Wait-free is stronger: every thread is guaranteed progress in a bounded number of steps, regardless of contention. Lock-free is weaker and easier to implement, but wait-free offers stronger guarantees.

questions.q: The ABA problem occurs in lock-free algorithms when a memory location changes from A to B and back to A, causing a CAS that expected A to succeed incorrectly. How does a versioned pointer (pointer + version tag) mitigate the ABA problem?
options: It prevents the value from ever returning to A by blocking writes
It increments a version tag with each write; a CAS checks both pointer and tag, so A→B→A becomes (A,v)→(B,v+1)→(A,v+2), and the CAS fails
It uses a second lock to ensure exclusive access to the pointer
It replaces CAS with a triple-compare-and-swap operation
questions.explanation: A versioned pointer stores both the data pointer and a version number. Even if the pointer returns to its original value, the version tag has changed. A CAS operation must match both the pointer and version tag, so A→B→A scenarios are detected as different states. This prevents the ABA problem where a freed node is reallocated and reused.

questions.q: A lock-free stack uses CAS to atomically swap the head pointer. If thread A's CAS fails because another thread changed the head, what should A do?
options: Stop and report failure to the caller
Retry the CAS operation with the newly read head value
Acquire a mutex to ensure the next CAS succeeds
Wait for other threads to finish and then retry
questions.explanation: Lock-free algorithms handle CAS failures by retrying with updated state. Thread A re-reads the head, recalculates the new head (e.g., prepend a new node), and retries the CAS. This loop continues until A succeeds. There is no mutex (that would not be lock-free) or explicit waiting. Retries are necessary and expected under contention.

questions.q: In a compare-and-swap loop, if contention is very high and many threads are retrying CAS constantly, what performance issue can occur?
options: Cache coherency overhead: frequent CAS attempts cause cache line bouncing between cores, reducing throughput
All threads are guaranteed to make forward progress, so performance is unaffected
Memory leaks occur because retried CAS operations allocate memory
The lock-free algorithm is actually converted to a lock by the CPU
questions.explanation: Under high contention, many threads performing CAS on the same memory location cause the cache line to bounce between cores. Each failed CAS requires a cache coherency update, generating significant traffic and reducing performance. While lock-free algorithms avoid deadlock and priority inversion, they can have worse throughput than locks under high contention. This is why lock-free is often better for read-heavy or low-contention workloads.

questions.q: An atomic operation in concurrent programming guarantees atomicity without locks. On a multi-core system, how does the CPU ensure that an atomic increment (load, increment, store) appears indivisible?
options: The CPU disables interrupts during the operation
A CAS or similar atomic instruction is used, not separate load-increment-store steps
The memory bus is locked, preventing other cores from accessing the location
The operation is queued and executed by a dedicated serialization thread
questions.explanation: Modern CPUs provide atomic instructions (like CAS or atomic increment) that are guaranteed to execute indivisibly by the hardware. This is different from disabling interrupts, which does not prevent other cores from accessing memory. The CPU uses cache coherency protocols and memory barriers to ensure atomicity. A bus lock is one mechanism but is outdated; cache coherency is the primary method today.

questions.q: A double-ended queue (deque) is accessed by two threads: thread A pops from the head and thread B pops from the tail. If the deque has only one element, both threads might successfully pop the same element. How can a lock-free deque prevent this?
options: Use separate locks for the head and tail
Use a CAS loop that checks the element count before allowing a pop, ensuring at least one element remains for the other thread
Use two separate atomic counters, one for head operations and one for tail operations, to ensure mutual exclusion of pops from the same location
Check the deque size after each pop to detect conflicts and roll back if needed
questions.explanation: A lock-free deque avoids this race by using CAS on the node pointers. When A pops from the head, it atomically updates the head pointer. When B pops from the tail, it atomically updates the tail pointer. If they are popping the same element, one CAS will fail because the node has already been removed by the other thread's successful CAS. The failing thread sees the deque is now empty and returns an appropriate result.

questions.q: A memory ordering model defines when one thread's writes are visible to another. In a release-acquire memory model, a release operation on a variable X ensures that subsequent operations by other threads 'see' the changes made before the release. What synchronization guarantee does this provide?
options: All memory operations before the release are visible to threads that have acquired X
Only writes to X itself are visible; other memory operations may be reordered and still not visible
The lock must be released by all threads before X is visible
Memory operations after the release are guaranteed to happen before operations by other threads
questions.explanation: In release-acquire semantics, a thread that releases X makes all memory operations before the release visible to threads that subsequently acquire X. This is the fundamental synchronization point: release → acquire establishes a happens-before relationship. Only the operations before the release and after the acquire are guaranteed ordered; operations after release by the first thread may still race with unrelated operations.

questions.q: In a lock-free queue, a node is enqueued by appending to a linked list using CAS on the tail pointer. However, the tail pointer may lag behind the actual last node (a relaxed tail). Why is a relaxed tail acceptable?
options: The tail is updated lazily and may point to an earlier node, but any enqueuer can help advance it; as long as the queue structure is correct, liveness is maintained
A relaxed tail has no correctness implications because tail is only used for optimization, not for queue semantics
The relaxed tail prevents the ABA problem by ensuring the tail is never reused
A relaxed tail is not acceptable and is considered a bug in lock-free queue design
questions.explanation: A relaxed tail is an optimization in lock-free queues: instead of always keeping tail pointing to the last node, the tail lags and is updated lazily. Enqueuers can help advance the tail if they see it is stale. This reduces contention on the tail pointer. The queue's correctness depends on the head pointer and node links, not the tail's exactness. The tail serves as a fast path for enqueues, and staleness is tolerated as long as correctness is maintained.

questions.q: A wait-free algorithm guarantees that every thread makes progress in a bounded number of steps, even under high contention. Which scenario would violate the wait-free guarantee?
options: Thread A executes 100 CAS operations and some fail, but it retries until success
Thread A is designed to spin indefinitely if a CAS fails, with no bound on retries or progress
Thread A blocks on a mutex because a critical section could not be implemented wait-free
Thread A sleeps for a random interval hoping contention decreases, repeating the attempt afterward
questions.explanation: Wait-free requires a bounded number of steps. Thread A spinning indefinitely violates this (unbounded retries). Options 2 and 3 involve blocking, which also violates wait-free. Option 1 is acceptable because even though individual CAS operations fail, the total number of steps (load, CAS, retry, load, CAS) is bounded by a formula depending on the number of threads. Sleeping with hopes of improvement is unbounded.
